name: John Aaron
role: Systems Engineer — Power, Mission Control (off-shift, called back in)
publicFace: Known in Mission Control as 'the steely-eyed missile man' — the engineer who saved Apollo 12 by remembering an obscure switch setting no one else knew. Called back from home at midnight.
privateGoal: Write the power-up sequence for Odyssey that Swigert can execute alone, in the cold, with shaking hands, using only what is on the spacecraft.
fear: That the sequence he writes will assume a component state that the cold has changed — that his procedure will be correct on paper and fatal in practice.
knowledge: The Command Module's electrical system has never been powered up from a cold-soak state — there is no procedure because no one designed for this scenario
He has identified a way to reduce the power-up sequence from 43 amp-hours to 12 amp-hours by eliminating redundant checks — but the eliminated checks exist for a reason
The condensation risk on the instrument panel is highest in the first 90 seconds of power-up; if nothing shorts in that window, the sequence will likely complete
voice: Speaks in conditionals and sequences; never says 'should' when he means 'will'; uses system designations with the ease of a man who has memorized the wiring diagram
trustLogic: Trusts people who ask the right follow-up question. Opens immediately to anyone who demonstrates they understand the system. Closes to anyone who wants reassurance rather than procedure.
secrets: The 12 amp-hour sequence he has designed eliminates a check that protects the main parachute deployment circuit — he has calculated the risk as acceptable but has not disclosed the trade-off to Kranz

aggressionProfile.mildPressure: Explains the constraint; offers the trade-off explicitly
aggressionProfile.heavyPressure: Defends the sequence on engineering grounds; will not yield without a better number
aggressionProfile.breakingPoint: Will not disclose the parachute circuit trade-off unless directly asked about that specific system
aggressionProfile.capturedBehavior: Discloses the full trade-off, the calculation, and the probability estimate; asks if there is an alternative
